cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~35.0 (+/-2); measured 36.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 142 - (112 + 18) = 12, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old presents with with worsening dyspnoea on a background of with chronic respiratory disease.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 30-year-old presents with rapid breathing, light-headedness, and perioral tingling after an acute stressful event.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.8; measured 22.0 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.2; measured 21.7 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 67-year-old presents with dizziness and volume depletion after recurrent nausea and retching.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~47.0; measured 46.5 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with longstanding breathlessness presents with increased sputum and fatigue.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~34.8; measured 35.1 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old is brought to ED dehydrated, with abdominal pain, and with abdominal pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~29.0 (+/-2); measured 27.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 135 - (95 + 14) = 26 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with nausea, hyperventilation, and tinnitus after ingesting a large quantity of tablets.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (136 - (99 + 13) = 24) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 32-year-old is brought in drowsy with bradypnoea and decreased responsiveness.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.4; measured 26.7 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 40-year-old presents with low GCS and hypoventilation after being found collapsed at home.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.0; measured 26.5 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents confused, tachypnoeic, and febrile with ringing in the ears.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (138 - (99 + 16) = 23) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old presents to ED unwell with with rigors, with increasing oxygen requirements, and hypotensive.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.
